card.indications: Prevention of kidney-transplant rejection and treatment of active rheumatoid arthritis when other therapy is inadequate; also used off-label for several autoimmune conditions.
card.mechanism: Converted to thiopurine metabolites that inhibit purine synthesis and lymphocyte proliferation.
card.dosing: Dose is weight based and individualized. TPMT and NUDT15 status, blood counts, liver function, kidney function, and interacting drugs may require major dose reduction.
card.routeAdministration: Give consistently with regard to meals. Tablets are hazardous drugs; avoid crushing unless proper precautions are used. IV use requires specialist preparation and monitoring.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Avoid use for rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy. Severe bone-marrow suppression or active serious infection may preclude therapy.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for malignancy with chronic immunosuppression. Severe myelosuppression, infection, hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, and fetal harm may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, fatigue, rash, and mild leukopenia.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe pancytopenia, opportunistic infection, lymphoma or skin cancer, hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, and severe hypersensitivity.
card.interactions: Allopurinol and febuxostat markedly increase toxicity; major dose reduction or avoidance is required. ACE inhibitors, warfarin, ribavirin, live vaccines, and other immunosuppressants interact.
card.monitoring: Monitor CBC with differential and platelets frequently during initiation, then periodically; liver enzymes, renal function, infection, bruising, and malignancy signs. Consider TPMT/NUDT15 testing.
card.patientTeaching: Report fever, sore throat, bruising, bleeding, jaundice, severe abdominal pain, or persistent vomiting. Avoid live vaccines and use sun protection.
card.nutrition: Take with food if nausea occurs, consistently each day. Avoid raw or undercooked foods when neutropenic and follow food-safety precautions.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible gram-negative bacterial infections; inhaled products are used in selected patients with cystic fibrosis and Pseudomonas infection.
card.mechanism: Binds penicillin-binding protein 3 and inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis, causing bacterial death.
card.dosing: IV or IM dosing varies by site and severity, commonly every 6 to 12 hours; renal impairment requires adjustment. Inhaled dosing follows product-specific cycles.
card.routeAdministration: Reconstitute and infuse according to product instructions. Do not mix incompatible drugs in the same line; flush shared tubing. Inhaled product requires the designated nebulizer.
card.contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity to aztreonam. Use caution in patients with prior severe beta-lactam allergy, especially ceftazidime allergy because of a similar side chain.
card.warnings: May cause anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, C. difficile-associated diarrhea, liver-enzyme elevation, and superinfection.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Injection-site discomfort, rash, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and transient liver-enzyme elevations; inhaled use may cause cough or wheeze.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Anaphylaxis, severe cutaneous reactions, C. difficile colitis, seizures with accumulation, serious liver injury, and bronchospasm with inhaled therapy.
card.interactions: Probenecid may increase levels modestly. Incompatible IV drugs should not be mixed. Live bacterial vaccines may be less effective.
card.monitoring: Monitor cultures, temperature, WBC count, renal and liver function, bowel pattern, allergy signs, infusion site, and respiratory response for inhaled therapy.
card.patientTeaching: Complete the full course. Report breathing difficulty, facial swelling, severe rash, or watery or bloody diarrhea. Use inhaled equipment exactly as trained.
card.nutrition: May be given without regard to food. Maintain hydration, especially with fever or diarrhea.

card.indications: Treatment of mildly to moderately active ulcerative colitis.
card.mechanism: Delivered to the colon where bacterial cleavage releases mesalamine, which reduces local inflammatory mediators.
card.dosing: Adult capsule regimens commonly use 2.25 g three times daily for a limited course. Pediatric and tablet dosing are product specific. Renal status must be considered.
card.routeAdministration: Swallow whole. Some capsules may be opened and sprinkled on applesauce according to labeling; swallow immediately without chewing.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to salicylates, aminosalicylates, balsalazide, or formulation ingredients.
card.warnings: Can cause renal impairment, mesalamine-induced acute intolerance syndrome, severe skin reactions, myocarditis or pericarditis, hepatotoxicity, and nephrolithiasis.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, respiratory infection, and arthralgia.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute kidney injury, severe colitis-like intolerance reaction, pancreatitis, hepatitis, blood dyscrasias, myocarditis, pericarditis, and severe skin reactions.
card.interactions: Nephrotoxic drugs increase renal risk. Azathioprine or 6-mercaptopurine can increase blood dyscrasias. Antacids may alter delivery of some products.
card.monitoring: Monitor stool frequency and blood, abdominal pain, renal function before and during therapy, CBC and liver tests when indicated, hydration, and hypersensitivity.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as prescribed and report worsening bloody diarrhea, severe cramps, fever, rash, chest pain, reduced urine, or flank pain.
card.nutrition: Maintain adequate fluid intake to reduce kidney-stone risk. Capsules may be sprinkled on applesauce only when the product instructions permit.

card.indications: Adjunct treatment of parkinsonism and acute extrapyramidal reactions caused by antipsychotic drugs. It is not effective for tardive dyskinesia.
card.mechanism: Blocks central muscarinic receptors and helps restore dopamine-acetylcholine balance in the basal ganglia.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized and usually started low. Acute dystonia may be treated parenterally, followed by short-term oral therapy. Older adults need lower doses.
card.routeAdministration: Oral doses may be taken once daily or divided. IM is generally preferred over IV when parenteral treatment is needed. Avoid abrupt discontinuation after prolonged use.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity and use in children under 3 years. Use caution with glaucoma, urinary retention, GI obstruction, tachycardia, and cognitive impairment.
card.warnings: Can cause confusion, delirium, hallucinations, heat stroke, anhidrosis, urinary retention, ileus, and worsening tardive dyskinesia.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, urinary hesitancy, tachycardia, nausea, drowsiness, and memory impairment.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe anticholinergic toxicity, paralytic ileus, acute angle-closure glaucoma, hyperthermia, psychosis, and urinary retention.
card.interactions: Other anticholinergics, antihistamines, tricyclics, antipsychotics, opioids, and alcohol increase anticholinergic or CNS effects.
card.monitoring: Monitor rigidity, tremor, dystonia, gait, cognition, bowel and bladder function, temperature, hydration, pulse, and eye symptoms.
card.patientTeaching: Avoid overheating and driving until effects are known. Report inability to urinate, severe constipation, eye pain, confusion, fever, or hallucinations.
card.nutrition: Increase fluids and fiber unless contraindicated. Sugar-free gum or candy may help dry mouth. Avoid alcohol and excessive heat.

card.indications: Oral treatment of hypertension and ophthalmic reduction of intraocular pressure in glaucoma or ocular hypertension.
card.mechanism: Selectively blocks beta1 receptors, lowering heart rate and blood pressure; in the eye it decreases aqueous humor production.
card.dosing: Oral dosing is commonly once daily and individualized. Ophthalmic dosing is commonly one drop twice daily, depending on product and response.
card.routeAdministration: For eye drops, apply punctal occlusion and avoid touching the tip. Check pulse and blood pressure with systemic therapy. Do not stop oral therapy abruptly.
card.contraindications: Sinus bradycardia, greater-than-first-degree heart block, cardiogenic shock, or overt cardiac failure. Ophthalmic use still carries systemic beta-blocker risks.
card.warnings: Can cause bradycardia, heart block, worsening heart failure, bronchospasm, hypotension, and masking of hypoglycemia.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Slow pulse, fatigue, dizziness, headache, eye irritation, tearing, blurred vision, and dry eyes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe bradycardia, AV block, bronchospasm, syncope, heart failure, and severe ocular hypersensitivity.
card.interactions: Other beta blockers, verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine, and glucose-lowering drugs can increase risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, pulse, heart-failure symptoms, respiratory status, glucose in diabetes, intraocular pressure, and eye irritation.
card.patientTeaching: Use punctal pressure after eye drops. Do not stop oral doses suddenly. Report wheezing, fainting, swelling, or a very slow pulse.
card.nutrition: No specific food restriction. Limit alcohol if dizziness occurs. Follow sodium guidance for hypertension and monitor glucose carefully if diabetic.
